metadata.title: Claude, Allowed.
metadata.thesis: The ban is a rule about homework. It is not a prophecy about you.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

beats.narration_text: Your school banned AI — but not your future. Three things: what the ban actually covers, what it does not, and the one rule that keeps you clean either way. Let us read the policy, not the headline.

props.command: my school banned AI — now what?
output: bans cover: graded work submitted without disclosure
bans don't cover: fluency practice on your own time
the safe rule: ask your teacher — get it in writing

beats.narration_text: Most school AI bans say roughly the same thing: do not use AI to do work you are supposed to do yourself and submit it without saying so. That is the scope — graded work, undisclosed. Not: do not ever use AI. Not: do not get fluent. Not: do not practice on your own time. Read the sentence, not the story around it.

props.heading: A rule, not a prophecy.
props.body:
What bans cover:
— graded work submitted without disclosure
— exams and assessments
— work evaluated as your own

What bans don't cover:
— fluency practice on your own time
— learning how AI works
— approved classroom tools

beats.narration_text: Here is where it gets slippery. Nearly every policy has an exception — something like 'except for educational purposes.' Teachers interpret this differently. One thinks it means any classroom use with permission. Another thinks it means nothing AI, full stop. You do not want to find out which one your teacher is after you have already submitted. The rule is: ask before you use, and get the answer in writing. Even an email reply counts. Assumptions here cost grade points.

props.heading: Never assume — ask.
props.body:
Vague phrase: 'except for educational purposes'

Interpretations vary:
— any classroom use with permission
— teacher-directed tools only
— nothing AI, full stop

Safe move: ask your teacher · get it in writing.

beats.narration_text: What is yours, regardless of any policy: fluency. You can use Claude on your own time to practice prompting, to quiz yourself on your own material, to get explanations for things you are confused about — as long as you are not submitting AI output as your own work. Anthropic's AI Fluency course is Creative Commons and free. A ban on AI in graded homework is not a ban on becoming capable.

props.heading: Practice is not cheating.
props.body:
Yours regardless of any policy:
— practicing prompting on your own time
— quizzing yourself on your own material
— AI Fluency course (CC, free)
— learning how the model reasons

A ban on graded homework ≠ a ban on becoming capable.

beats.narration_text: Before the verdict — one question to commit to: what actually gets students caught? The use itself — or something else? Think before the next beat flips.

props.command: what actually gets students caught — using AI, or something else?

beats.narration_text: The answer: it is not the use. It is the undisclosed use. Students caught for AI violations are not punished for being AI-fluent. They are punished for submitting AI-assisted work without saying so. Same output — different path. Disclosure does not make the work automatically fine, but it puts you in the conversation instead of outside it.

props.heading: Disclosure is the shield.
props.body:
Using AI: low risk when disclosed.
Undisclosed AI use: the actual violation.

Same output — different path.
Disclosure puts you in the conversation, not outside it.

beats.narration_text: So the policy collapses to one question: did you tell your teacher? That is it. A rule about disclosure, applied to graded work. The working world you are walking into runs on AI. The ban is about this semester's homework. Getting fluent — legally, on your own time, with disclosure — is not cheating. It is preparation.

artifactLines: Bans cover: graded work submitted without disclosure — not fluency, not practice on your own time.
The exemption trap: 'educational purposes' means different things to different teachers — ask first, get it in writing.
What's yours: AI Fluency course (CC, free), home practice, ungraded skill-building — a ban doesn't ban becoming capable.
The one rule: disclose. A disclosed use is a conversation. An undisclosed one is a violation.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Do not assume — ask. Paste this into Claude with your actual assignment. You will get a short honest email to send your teacher. The reply tells you what is actually allowed in your context, and puts you on the right side of the policy before you start.

props.command: Draft me a short, honest email to my teacher asking whether I can use Claude for [describe your specific assignment or subject]. I want to start the right conversation — keep it one paragraph.

beats.narration_text: Claude, Allowed. If this cleared the fog, subscribe. HAI.
